C[HistoryTable: I discovered Go last year and it's quickly become one of my favorite games
C[Bray: Kinda crazy how good this game is. Endless frustration and fun all in one package.
Bray: Thank you for the game.
Bray: That shape at the top-right is called a ""bulky five"". That would be a good one to look up, as it will be related to similar life-and-death issues as well.

HistoryTable: I appreciate that, I started life and death when I first gone in but have gotten away from it. I was aware that the game was over a while ago but wanted to see how the various situations played out, hope that's ok

Bray: It's completely fine. Especially those corner shapes. You need to see them happen, get game experience, practice with L&D, repeat endlessly. Life and Death never ends it just build on easier problems to make harder ones.
Bray: You could have started a ko in the bottom-right, but it would have been a tough one to win.

Bray: OK, yeah. The AI is suggesting that as well. When you see it suggest T2 for move 211 that's the ko. I would suggest probably ignoring that for now and make sure you understand why the top-right and bottom-left groups are dead. Those are both fundamental shapes.

C[LindyHiker: Hm. Probably should have kept going a bit more. Thanks for the game! Seems likely that I would have continued my downward trend into a full loss as I got the worst of each fight, but resigning will still in a better position isn't great.
Bray: We've all been there before. You played it well. Yes, the AI playing at god level says that black is ahead, but at our level who knows.
Bray: White in a 6-stone game is on a constant treadmill of scratching back points. I certainly have no idea if I could have grabbed the last ~20 in the endgame or not.
Bray: My best guess was that it was going to be very close.

C[Bray: I'd never suggest that a beginner learn joseki, but you'll certainly run into 3-3 invasions a lot when you play 4-4, especially post-AlphaGo.

Bray: There are many variations, but the main thing to keep in mind is that there's a trade going on. One side is getting territory, the other side is gaining outside influence. This is generally true of every opening pattern.
Bray: A 4-4 stone without support can't kill an invading 3-3 stone. It's simply not possible barring mistakes or something else going on on the board. So it becomes a negotiation and a trade.

JuanPabloMedina: One thing led me to another and then I got to “haikus” and then to go
C[Bray: Well there's certainly a lot of beauty in this game.
C[JuanPabloMedina: I hace a question, is this move make an eye on your side?
Bray: It does make an eye at H4
JuanPabloMedina: I dont understand very well the diferente between an eye and a false eye
C[Bray: If black managed to get a living stone at both J2 and J3 then the eye at H4 would become false, because at some point black could just capture H3.
Bray: A real eye means that there's really nothing your opponent can do to remove it, assume that you're responding to his moves.
JuanPabloMedina: Now it is not possible for me to approach the false eye because of f3 right?

Bray: Any time black would play J2, white would have the option to play J3 in response. In this particular game maybe that wouldn't happen, because the white group is already alive. But in general that's why I could say that H4 is an eye.

C[Bray: Maybe J2 is a ko threat, but I wouldn't worry about that for now. Ko is super complicated, and not really something to worry about. When they happen in your games, just do your best at the time.
C[JuanPabloMedina: :-( i wanted to do so in orden to connect my groups
Bray: You have one group left (the upper-left). The question for you is how to make it live.
C[JuanPabloMedina: Should i think on making an eye perhaps?
Bray: It's actually a good puzzle now.
Bray: I believe that black is dead in the upper-left now, but I'm trying to think through some options.
Bray: B4 and C9 have squeezed black into an area that might be too small to make 2 eyes
C[JuanPabloMedina: I dos not ser that ☺️ , I’m very beginner
C[Bray: Oh that never goes away. There's a video floating around of a pro playing a self-atari. Sometimes it just happens.
Bray: If a 9-dan pro can do it, we don't need to feel bad about it.
JuanPabloMedina: Playing J5 would be the same false eye right?
Bray: It's starting a ko fight, so we're going to ignore that. Remember when I said that white could kill the group by playing at H9?
Bray: ""My opponent's good move is my good move.""

C[Bray: If white plays H9, the eye at J8 becomes false, because that stone at J9 is captured later. I would suggest that you undo that last move and look for another answer. The black group can live, but with J9 there it's dead.
C[Bray: What happens if white gets to play J6?
C[JuanPabloMedina: You can erase the eye with the next move?
Bray: White J6 makes the eye false and kills the black group.

Bray: You can simply pass if you like. There aren't any points left on the board.

C[Bray: You will get a lot of value from life-and-death problems. If you click ""puzzles"" at the top of the page looks for ""Exercises for Beginners"" from ""mark5000"". Should be near the top.

C[Bray: I'd never suggest that a beginner learn joseki, but you'll certainly run into 3-3 invasions a lot when you play 4-4, especially post-AlphaGo.

Bray: There are many variations, but the main thing to keep in mind is that there's a trade going on. One side is getting territory, the other side is gaining outside influence. This is generally true of every opening pattern.
Bray: A 4-4 stone without support can't kill an invading 3-3 stone. It's simply not possible barring mistakes or something else going on on the board. So it becomes a negotiation and a trade.
